satisfaction.reasoning: The headline tells you exactly what the product is rather than creating any tension that pulls you into the next line — it resolves before it even starts. There's also no social proof woven anywhere into the copy, which is a real missed opportunity given how trust-dependent the supplement space is.

The headline tension (H1) is genuinely working, and the voice is solid throughout. The two gaps pulling the score down are the absence of any woven-in social proof (H2) and a CTA that names what you get but doesn't quite feel like the inevitable next step from the body copy (H4).

Almost everything is landing — the headline tension, the CTA flow, the benefits translation are all solid. The one gap is that there's no customer voice woven into the copy anywhere; the whole page is the brand talking about itself, and a real person's words somewhere in the benefits block would make the argument feel lived-in rather than just well-written.

Every hidden preference is fully met — the headline creates genuine tension, the customer quotes are woven into the narrative at exactly the right moments, every feature earns its 'so what,' the CTA flows naturally from the copy, and there's one clean primary action throughout. No dealbreakers, voice is consistent and on-brand.

The hook and CTA are both strong, and the voice is exactly right. But the testimonial feels a little marooned — it sits between two paragraphs without really connecting to the point being made, so it reads more like a proof block that got moved than a natural part of the story.

H2 is now working well — the quote earns its place and the fix landed. But I'm only seeing a portion of the page, so H3, H4, and H5 are partially scored based on what's visible; the middle section doesn't do enough work translating what the product actually does into why I should care beyond the emotional texture.

The middle section fix worked — the concrete benefits are finally landing and the quote seam is gone. But this is a middle section in isolation: there's no headline creating tension, no CTA flowing from the copy, and the quote is still slightly floating after the paragraph rather than sitting inside it where the emotional confirmation lands hardest.

H2 and H3 are working really well now, and the quote fix landed exactly right. The main gap is H4 and H5 — the closing line sets up a feeling but doesn't actually complete the action loop, and there's no visible CTA in this draft to evaluate whether the one-action principle is being honored.

The quote placement and CTA are fully working now, and the tone is exactly right throughout. The one thing that's still nagging me is H3 — the copy names the problems really well (redness, tightness, the reactive cycle) but it doesn't quite close the loop on why the solution works, which means the benefit lands a little soft.

The middle section is genuinely working now — the mechanism sentence landed, Renee's quote is fully earned, and the closing image on the Tuesday mirror moment is the best line on the page. But this draft is still missing a CTA entirely, and H4 and H5 can't be evaluated without one — which means we're looking at an incomplete page, not a near-finished one.

Every preference landed — the headline creates genuine tension, Renee's quote is structurally earned by the open paragraph, the feature-to-benefit translation is concrete and specific, the CTA flows as a natural exhale, and there's exactly one action on the page. No dealbreakers anywhere.

The headline tension and single CTA are strong, but the social proof is sitting at the bottom like a testimonials block rather than living inside the story where it would actually do work. The CTA also almost lands but the transition into it feels slightly abrupt.

Almost everything is working — the headline tension, the embedded Priya quote, the single CTA are all landing. The one place that's slightly soft is the transition into the CTA; 'You've already done the hard part' is a nice line but it doesn't quite close the loop on what you're feeling when you arrive at 'Get your link' — it's close, but it doesn't fully earn the click.

Every preference landed — the headline creates genuine tension, Priya is structural not decorative, the CTA flows out of the narrative rather than interrupting it, and there's exactly one action on the page. No dealbreakers anywhere.

The headline and feature-to-benefit translation are genuinely strong, and the voice is exactly right. The main issue is that the customer quote lands in a weird spot — it shows up after the copy has already made its point and before the CTA, which makes it feel tacked on rather than woven in. The CTA also doesn't quite earn itself; the line before it ('The desk sits at whatever height you left it. It remembers.') is a nice detail but it's too small a note to close on — the momentum just quietly disappears instead of pulling you forward.

Almost everything is working — the headline, the quote placement, the feature-to-benefit translation are all landing. The one thing that's slightly off is the CTA setup: 'It remembers where you left it. Turns out that's all it takes.' is a good closing thought, but it doesn't quite pull you forward into the action — it wraps up the argument a little too neatly, and then the CTA has to do the last bit of work alone.

Every preference landed — the headline creates real tension, the Renata quote is doing structural work inside the argument rather than sitting at the bottom like a trophy, features are translated into benefits throughout, and the CTA flows from the open thread above it rather than arriving as a separate ask. No dealbreakers anywhere.
